reason: The item cleanly tests admissible inference from benchmarked protocol/metric-dependent errors plus a named structural risk mechanism, while rewarding boundary-qualified selection logic. Decoys are largely single-fault and engineering-plausible (monotonicity overreach, cross-protocol rank transfer, class-to-instance overgeneralization), though two rely on similar “overreach beyond evidence scope,” slightly softening discriminative sharpness.

The prompt elicits a single coherent expert judgment: decomposing an abnormally large resistance rise into coupled thermal and electrochemical overcharge mechanisms, then using the 0.1C contrast to apportion contribution and articulate a concrete diagnostic limitation. Boundary conditions are mostly tight (magnitude-only resistance cannot separate over-discharge vs low-temperature; DOD-dependence is needed), and the 0.1C experiment provides a clean perturbation that discourages hand-wavy explanations. The main quality risk is that, as a short-answer item without explicit decoy options, scoring can be sensitive to phrasing and the “two signatures” mapping, which slightly reduces single-fault purity and discriminative robustness versus a multiple-choice format.
